prior_summary_gold:
Round 1 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P4 | Vote: Unanimous approval | Outcome: FAIL

P3 immediately pointed to the mission members as suspect and urged excluding both P1 and P4 from future teams, while P2 suggested building a fresh team with P2, P3, and P5. P5 accused P4 of sabotaging the quest, prompting P4 to deny responsibility and shift blame entirely onto P1. The failed mission left P1 and P4 both under heavy suspicion, with P3's call to exclude them gaining traction and P4's defensive counterattack likely to intensify scrutiny in the next round.
discussion_log:
Discussion after Quest 2:

P4: "Okay, can we stop pretending the common factor isn't P1? They were on the first fail and then they vote NO on the second team—classic evil posture without taking risk."

P3: "Facts: Quest 1 team was P1/P4 and it failed; Quest 2 team was P2/P3/P5 and it failed. That means at least one evil in {P1,P4} and at least one evil in {P2,P3,P5}, and since P2 and I weren't on Quest 1, the overlap points hard at P4 and P5 being the pair."

P5: "Hold on—P2 and P3 both approved my team and it still failed, so one of them has to be the saboteur, not me by default. Also P4 has been consistently calling out P1 since last round, which lines up with what we saw on Quest 1."

P2: "My vote was yes because the proposal avoided both P1 and P4 like we all agreed after the first fail, so it was the most rational test available. Now that it still failed with P5 on it, the cleanest explanation is P5 is evil and the other evil is in the Quest 1 pair—so I'm leaning P4 over P1."

discussion_log:
Discussion after Quest 3:

P4: "Three fails with three different-looking teams should tell you this wasn't some neat ‘P4/P5 conspiracy’—it’s whoever keeps ending up in the center of the trust circle, and that’s been P1 and P3 by how everyone keeps auto-approving around them." 

P5: "I’m not going to overcomplicate it: Quest 1 failed with P1/P4, Quest 2 failed with P2/P3/me, Quest 3 failed with P1/P4 again—if you want two names that cover all three results, it’s P1 plus one of P2 or P3." 

P3: "Let’s do the math cleanly: Q1 (P1,P4) failed and Q3 (P1,P4) failed again, so unless you believe P1 double-sabotaged both times, the consistent evil there is P4. Q2 (P2,P3,P5) failed with P2 and me off the other two fails, so the other evil is P5—my final call is P4 and P5." 

P2: "Evidence-wise, only two people appear in multiple failed missions together: P1 and P4, and both those missions failed, which makes P4 the simplest constant. Then Q2 is the only place the second evil can live, and with P3’s reasoning lining up, I’m also locking in P4 and P5 as the pair."
